clinicalStem: A 52-year-old fair-skinned man presents to the plastic surgery outpatient clinic with a 9-month history of a changing pigmented lesion on his upper back. He reports that the lesion has grown and that a portion of it has lightened in colour. On examination the lesion measures 14 mm at its widest diameter; it is asymmetric with an irregular, notched border, has areas of dark brown, black, and a central zone of pale regression, and is slightly raised. There is no palpable regional lymphadenopathy. He has no personal or family history of melanoma. Which single investigation is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Incisional punch biopsy of the darkest area
Wide local excision with 1 cm margins
Fine-needle aspiration cytology of the lesion
Shave biopsy flush with surrounding skin
explanation: For a pigmented lesion suspicious for melanoma, excision biopsy with narrow (2 mm) clinical margins is the gold-standard diagnostic procedure. It provides the complete lesional architecture needed for accurate Breslow thickness measurement, which drives all subsequent management decisions. Wide local excision is the definitive treatment margin and is not performed until histological confirmation and Breslow depth are known. Incisional, shave, and FNA techniques risk sampling error or destruction of the depth measurement.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Narrow-margin excision biopsy is the preferred method for suspected melanoma, preserving the entire lesion for histopathological assessment including Breslow depth.
Incisional biopsy risks sampling error by missing the deepest component of the tumour, which would underestimate Breslow thickness and lead to under-staging.
Wide local excision is the definitive therapeutic excision performed after histological diagnosis is confirmed; it is not the diagnostic step.
FNA cytology cannot provide tissue architecture or Breslow depth, making it unsuitable as a primary diagnostic tool for a primary pigmented lesion.
Shave biopsy transects the deep margin of the lesion, making accurate Breslow thickness measurement impossible and potentially leaving tumour in situ.
clinicalPearl: Always perform a narrow-margin excision biopsy for any lesion suspicious for melanoma — Breslow thickness from the biopsy specimen dictates all subsequent surgical margins and sentinel node decisions.
keyClue: Changing 14 mm pigmented lesion with asymmetry, irregular border, multi-colour (brown/black/regression white) — classic high-risk features requiring diagnostic excision.
commonExamTrap: Candidates often select wide local excision (1 cm margins) as the answer, confusing the diagnostic step with the definitive therapeutic step.
missedConcept: Breslow thickness can only be accurately measured on a complete excision specimen; any biopsy that transects the base invalidates staging.

clinicalStem: A 44-year-old woman undergoes excision biopsy of a pigmented lesion from her left calf. Histology confirms superficial spreading melanoma, Breslow thickness 1.8 mm, Clark level IV, no ulceration, mitotic rate 1/mm², margins clear. She is otherwise well with no palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy and a normal chest X-ray. Which investigation is most appropriate to guide further surgical management?
options: MRI of the left lower limb
Ultrasound of the left inguinal nodes
explanation: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is indicated for melanomas with Breslow thickness ≥1 mm (or 0.75–1 mm with high-risk features). A 1.8 mm non-ulcerated melanoma with Clark IV clearly meets this threshold. SLNB provides accurate nodal staging that determines prognosis and eligibility for adjuvant therapy, and cannot be replaced by imaging in clinically node-negative disease because microscopic nodal deposits are below CT/PET resolution. Ultrasound can detect macroscopic nodal disease but not micrometastases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. SLNB is the standard of care for staging clinically node-negative melanoma ≥1 mm Breslow thickness.
CT staging is reserved for Stage III/IV disease or high-risk features; in a clinically node-negative Stage IIB patient the yield is very low and it does not replace SLNB for nodal staging.
PET-CT has high sensitivity for distant metastatic disease but is not indicated for initial staging of early-stage melanoma without clinical or CT evidence of systemic spread.
MRI of the limb evaluates local soft-tissue extent but does not provide nodal or distant staging information relevant at this stage.
Ultrasound can detect macroscopic lymph node disease but has insufficient sensitivity for the micrometastases that SLNB is designed to detect; it does not replace SLNB.
clinicalPearl: Sentinel lymph node biopsy is the most important staging investigation for melanoma ≥1 mm Breslow thickness with clinically negative nodes — it detects micrometastases invisible to all imaging modalities.
keyClue: Breslow 1.8 mm, clinically node-negative — the thickness alone triggers SLNB indication.
commonExamTrap: Choosing CT or PET-CT, which are useful for distant staging but cannot replace SLNB for nodal micrometastasis detection.
missedConcept: Imaging cannot replace SLNB for staging clinically node-negative melanoma because micrometastases are below the resolution of all current imaging.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old Afro-Caribbean man is referred by his GP with a 2-year history of a slowly enlarging dark irregular streak under the nail of his right great toe. He denies trauma to the area. Examination reveals a longitudinal melanonychia measuring 7 mm in width involving more than two thirds of the nail plate, with extension of pigmentation onto the proximal nail fold (Hutchinson's sign). He is otherwise healthy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Hutchinson's sign — pigmentation spreading from the nail bed onto the proximal nail fold — is pathognomonic of subungual melanoma (acral lentiginous subtype) until proven otherwise. Acral lentiginous melanoma is the most common melanoma subtype in dark-skinned individuals and characteristically arises on the plantar surface, palms, or subungually. The width >3 mm, involvement of >2/3 nail plate, and lack of trauma history further support malignancy over benign longitudinal melanonychia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Hutchinson's sign combined with wide, irregular nail-plate pigmentation in a dark-skinned patient is the hallmark presentation of subungual acral lentiginous melanoma.
Subungual haematoma is typically dark purple-black, has a proximal advancing free edge as the nail grows, and does not spread onto the proximal nail fold (no Hutchinson's sign).
Benign ethnic melanonychia produces narrow, uniform, evenly pigmented bands without Hutchinson's sign; it does not typically widen or become irregular over time.
Subungual SCC can cause nail destruction but does not produce melanin-based longitudinal pigmentation or Hutchinson's sign.
Onychomycosis causes nail thickening, discoloration (yellow/white/brown), and dystrophy but not a pigmented longitudinal streak with periungual extension.
clinicalPearl: Hutchinson's sign (periungual pigmentation extending onto the proximal nail fold) is a red flag for subungual melanoma and demands urgent biopsy regardless of ethnicity.
keyClue: Hutchinson's sign — pigment extending onto the proximal nail fold — is the diagnostic key clue.
commonExamTrap: Attributing longitudinal melanonychia to benign ethnic pigmentation in a dark-skinned patient, thereby missing a subungual melanoma.
missedConcept: Hutchinson's sign effectively excludes benign causes and mandates biopsy; no benign condition reliably produces periungual extension of pigment.

clinicalStem: A 71-year-old retired farmer presents to the plastic surgery clinic with a 3-year history of a slowly growing lesion on the left side of his nose. He spent most of his working life outdoors. Examination reveals a pearly, translucent papule 8 mm in diameter with a rolled, telangiectatic border and a central area of superficial ulceration ("rodent ulcer" appearance). There is no regional lymphadenopathy. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The classic triad of a pearly papule with a rolled telangiectatic border and central ulceration on a sun-exposed site (nose) in an elderly patient with a history of chronic UV exposure is pathognomonic of nodular BCC. BCC is the most common skin cancer and preferentially arises on the face (nose most common single site). The 'rodent ulcer' descriptor specifically refers to a centrally ulcerated nodular BCC.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Pearly rolled telangiectatic border with central ulceration on the nose in a chronically sun-exposed patient is the textbook presentation of nodular BCC.
SCC typically presents as an indurated, firm, keratinised nodule or ulcer with a raised everted edge; it lacks the pearly telangiectatic rolled border characteristic of BCC.
Keratoacanthoma grows rapidly over weeks (not years), presents as a crater-shaped nodule with a central keratin plug, and often regresses spontaneously; it is not pearly.
A sebaceous cyst has a central punctum, is fluctuant, and does not have a telangiectatic rolled border or ulcerate in this characteristic manner.
An intradermal naevus may be skin-coloured and slightly raised but does not ulcerate, does not have a rolled border, and is not associated with telangiectasia in this pattern.
clinicalPearl: The combination of pearly appearance, rolled telangiectatic border, and central ulceration on a sun-exposed facial site is diagnostic of nodular BCC — the most common form of the most common skin cancer.
keyClue: Pearly translucent papule with rolled telangiectatic border and central ulceration on the nose — the 'rodent ulcer' appearance.
commonExamTrap: Confusing with SCC because of the ulceration; the key differentiator is the pearly rolled telangiectatic border, which is unique to BCC.
missedConcept: The pearly/translucent quality and telangiectatic rolled border distinguish BCC from all other ulcerating skin lesions.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old renal transplant recipient on long-term ciclosporin and prednisolone presents with a rapidly enlarging, firm, indurated ulcerated nodule on the right temple that has grown over 6 weeks. The lesion measures 2.5 cm, has an everted edge, and bleeds easily on contact. He reports paraesthesia along the right side of his forehead. There is a 1 cm firm, mobile right preauricular lymph node. Which investigation is most appropriate for this patient?
options: Excision biopsy with histology and CT of the neck and parotid
Punch biopsy of the lesion only
Ultrasound of the right parotid gland alone
MRI of the skull base without nodal imaging
explanation: This is a high-risk SCC (immunosuppressed patient, >2 cm, rapid growth, palpable regional node, perineural invasion suggested by paraesthesia). Excision biopsy with full histology confirms diagnosis and grade, while CT of neck and parotid is required to stage nodal disease and detect perineural spread toward the skull base. High-risk SCC in immunosuppressed patients carries a significantly elevated risk of nodal metastasis and perineural invasion compared to immunocompetent patients; the palpable node and paraesthesia demand concurrent staging imaging.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. High-risk SCC features (immunosuppression, size >2 cm, palpable node, perineural symptoms) mandate both tissue diagnosis and nodal/perineural staging simultaneously.
Shave biopsy gives only a superficial sample and cannot assess tumour depth or perineural invasion; alone it is wholly insufficient for a high-risk lesion.
Punch biopsy alone provides histological diagnosis but no staging information; the palpable node and neurological symptom require concurrent imaging that cannot be deferred.
Ultrasound of the parotid alone misses the perineural spread toward the skull base suggested by paraesthesia and does not provide deep nodal staging.
MRI skull base without nodal imaging evaluates perineural extension but misses the regional nodal disease evidenced by the palpable preauricular node.
clinicalPearl: Perineural invasion (paraesthesia) plus regional lymphadenopathy in a high-risk SCC patient demands simultaneous tissue biopsy AND cross-sectional neck/parotid imaging — these cannot be staged sequentially.
keyClue: Immunosuppressed patient + palpable regional lymph node + paraesthesia (perineural invasion) = high-risk SCC requiring combined diagnostic biopsy and staging CT.
commonExamTrap: Selecting biopsy alone without imaging, not recognising that palpable nodes and perineural symptoms mandate concurrent staging in high-risk SCC.
missedConcept: Immunosuppression dramatically elevates SCC aggressiveness and nodal metastasis risk; perineural invasion in SCC requires skull-base imaging, not just local biopsy.

clinicalStem: A 76-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of a slowly enlarging, erythematous, scaly, well-defined plaque on her left shin. The plaque is 3 cm, flat, has sharp margins, is slightly crusted, and does not respond to topical antifungal cream. There is no induration, no ulceration, and no regional lymphadenopathy. She takes no immunosuppressants and has no history of arsenic exposure. Dermoscopy shows a dotted vessel pattern with surface scale. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bowen's disease (squamous cell carcinoma in situ)
explanation: Bowen's disease (SCC in situ) classically presents as a persistent, well-demarcated, erythematous, scaly plaque on the lower leg of elderly women that does not respond to antifungal or steroid treatment. The dotted vessel pattern on dermoscopy is characteristic. The absence of ulceration and induration indicates in-situ disease. The chronic non-healing course and failure of antifungal therapy effectively exclude tinea/eczema, and the flat non-pigmented nature distinguishes it from superficial spreading melanoma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. A persistent erythematous scaly well-demarcated shin plaque in an elderly woman unresponsive to antifungals, with dotted vessels on dermoscopy, is Bowen's disease until proven otherwise.

Superficial BCC also appears as a flat erythematous scaly plaque but typically has a subtle thread-like rolled border and is more common on the trunk; dermoscopy shows superficial fine telangiectasia rather than dotted vessels.

Psoriatic plaques are usually multiple, bilateral, have silvery scales, affect classical sites (elbows, scalp, knees), and typically show other features of psoriasis; a single isolated shin plaque unresponsive to antifungal therapy favours Bowen's disease.

Discoid eczema is intensely itchy, often weeping or crusted, responds to topical steroids, and occurs in a bilateral distribution; a solitary non-itchy persistent plaque unresponsive to antifungals is not consistent.
Superficial spreading melanoma is pigmented (brown/black) rather than purely erythematous and shows atypical pigment network on dermoscopy rather than dotted vessels.
clinicalPearl: A single persistent erythematous scaly plaque on the lower leg of an elderly woman that fails to respond to antifungal therapy is Bowen's disease until histology proves otherwise — biopsy is mandatory.
keyClue: Persistent, well-demarcated erythematous scaly plaque, lower leg, elderly woman, unresponsive to antifungals, dotted vessels on dermoscopy.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing discoid eczema or psoriasis and treating with steroids, thereby delaying biopsy and diagnosis of in-situ SCC.
missedConcept: Bowen's disease is SCC in situ; untreated it has approximately 3–5% risk of progression to invasive SCC, making early diagnosis important.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man presents to the surgical outpatient clinic with a 6-month history of a painless swelling on the back of his neck. On examination there is a 3 cm smooth, spherical, fluctuant swelling that is non-tender, transilluminates poorly, and moves freely with the overlying skin. A small central punctum is visible. There is no erythema or discharge. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: A sebaceous (epidermoid) cyst is characterised by the central punctum, skin tethering (it moves with the overlying skin), smooth spherical fluctuant consistency, and no transillumination. Lipomas are subcutaneous, move independently of overlying skin, lack a punctum, and are lobulated rather than spherical. The central punctum is the pathognomonic distinguishing feature of an epidermoid cyst.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Central punctum + skin tethering + smooth fluctuant swelling = epidermoid (sebaceous) cyst. This is the classic presentation.
A lipoma lies in the subcutaneous fat, moves freely without skin tethering, does not have a punctum, and feels soft and lobulated ('slip sign') rather than tense and spherical.
A dermoid cyst is a developmental cyst found at lines of embryonic fusion (midline forehead, outer eyebrow, midline neck); it is not tethered to overlying skin and does not have a punctum.
Ganglion cysts arise in association with joints or tendon sheaths (dorsum of wrist most commonly), transilluminate brightly, and are not tethered to skin.

Pilomatrixoma (calcifying epithelioma of Malherbe) is a firm-to-hard, sometimes lobulated subcutaneous nodule typically seen in children and young adults; it does not have a punctum and has a characteristic 'tent sign' or irregular hard consistency.
clinicalPearl: The central punctum is the single most reliable clinical sign that identifies an epidermoid (sebaceous) cyst and differentiates it from all other subcutaneous swellings.
keyClue: Central punctum + skin tethering (moves with overlying skin).
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing lipoma because the swelling is smooth and non-tender, missing the punctum and skin tethering that define the epidermoid cyst.
missedConcept: Epidermoid cysts are tethered to the dermis (skin moves with them) while lipomas are freely mobile within the subcutaneous plane independently of overlying skin.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old male farmer presents with a 6-week history of a rapidly growing nodule on the dorsum of his left hand. On examination there is a firm, dome-shaped nodule measuring 18 mm with a central keratin-filled crater. It appeared suddenly 2 months ago, grew rapidly for 4 weeks, and appears to be stabilising. He has no lymphadenopathy. He is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Clinical observation for 3 months to assess for spontaneous regression
Topical imiquimod cream trial for 6 weeks
Shave biopsy of the crater rim
Punch biopsy of the base only
explanation: Although keratoacanthoma (KA) classically presents with a dome-shaped crater-form nodule that grows rapidly and may regress, histological differentiation from well-differentiated SCC is often impossible without full architectural assessment of the lesion. Current best practice mandates excision biopsy rather than watchful waiting, because relying on clinical diagnosis alone risks under-treating an invasive SCC. A shave or partial biopsy cannot provide the architecture needed to distinguish KA from SCC histologically.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Despite the classic KA appearance, excision biopsy is mandatory because KA cannot be reliably distinguished from well-differentiated SCC on clinical grounds or partial sampling alone.
Watchful waiting for spontaneous regression carries an unacceptable risk if the lesion is actually an invasive SCC, which can metastasise during the observation period.
Topical imiquimod has been described for KA but is not appropriate as initial management for a rapidly growing lesion that cannot be distinguished from SCC without histology.
Shave biopsy removes only the surface of the crater but not the full tumour base, destroying the architectural relationship needed to diagnose KA versus SCC.
Punch biopsy of the base alone provides only a small core sample and loses the interface between normal skin and tumour that is required for the architectural diagnosis of KA versus SCC.
clinicalPearl: Keratoacanthoma cannot be clinically or histologically distinguished from well-differentiated SCC on partial biopsy — excision biopsy is mandatory for all crater-form nodules.
keyClue: Rapid growth over weeks + dome-shaped crater with central keratin plug = classic KA appearance, but excision biopsy is still required to exclude SCC.
commonExamTrap: Choosing observation because the rapid growth and central crater are 'classic for KA'; this is dangerous because SCC cannot be excluded without full excision histology.
missedConcept: The differentiation of keratoacanthoma from well-differentiated SCC requires full architectural assessment of the entire lesion; partial biopsy and clinical features alone are insufficient.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a firm, slightly pigmented papule on her right thigh that has been present for 2 years without significant change. She recalls a possible insect bite at the same site years ago. On examination the lesion is 8 mm, brownish, slightly raised, firm, and tethered to the dermis. When the skin around the lesion is pinched, it dimples inward (positive dimple/Fitzpatrick sign). There is no ulceration and no regional lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The Fitzpatrick dimple sign — inward dimpling of a lesion when the surrounding skin is pinched — is pathognomonic of dermatofibroma, caused by the lesion's tethering to the dermis. Combined with the firm, slightly pigmented nature, the history of possible insect bite, benign course over years, and typical location on the thigh of a young woman, dermatofibroma is the definitive diagnosis. DFSP would be larger, rapidly growing, and show a different dermoscopic pattern.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Positive Fitzpatrick dimple sign + firm dermal papule + slow stable course + prior trauma history = dermatofibroma.
A melanocytic naevus is typically soft, not tethered to dermis, and does not show the Fitzpatrick dimple sign; it also does not usually follow a traumatic trigger.
Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans is locally aggressive, grows progressively, is typically larger at presentation, feels indurated and multi-nodular, and does not show a simple dimple sign on pinching.
A neurofibroma is soft and compressible (not firm), may show a 'button-hole sign' where it invaginates on palpation, and is not specifically tethered to the dermis in the same way.
Nodular melanoma grows rapidly over weeks to months, is deeply pigmented or amelanotic, and often ulcerates; a 2-year stable course effectively excludes this diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: The Fitzpatrick dimple sign (inward dimpling on lateral pinching) is pathognomonic of dermatofibroma and reliably distinguishes it from other dermal papules.
keyClue: Positive Fitzpatrick dimple sign on lateral pinching of a firm dermal papule on the thigh.
commonExamTrap: Confusing with DFSP because both are firm and pigmented; DFSP does not show the dimple sign and is larger and locally destructive.
missedConcept: The Fitzpatrick sign reflects the mechanical tethering of dermatofibroma to the dermis; no other common skin lesion shows this sign reliably.

topic: Infantile haemangioma vs vascular malformation — diagnosis
clinicalStem: A 4-month-old girl is brought by her parents who are concerned about a raised, bright red, soft lesion on her right cheek that was absent at birth but appeared at 2 weeks of age and has been growing rapidly ever since. On examination it is a well-defined, 3 cm, bright red, soft, lobulated strawberry-coloured lesion that blanches on pressure and feels warm. There are no overlying skin changes suggestive of ulceration. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Infantile haemangiomas are absent at birth (or show a faint precursor mark), appear in the first few weeks of life, and undergo a characteristic growth phase in the first year. The bright red, lobulated, soft, warm, compressible 'strawberry' appearance with rapid postnatal growth is classic. Vascular malformations, by contrast, are present at birth, grow proportionally with the child, and do not involute. The key distinction is postnatal onset with rapid growth in infancy, which is unique to haemangiomas.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Absent at birth, appears at 2 weeks, rapid postnatal growth, bright red lobulated warm compressible lesion = infantile haemangioma.
Capillary malformations (port-wine stains) are present at birth as flat pink-red macular lesions that do not grow rapidly; they are not raised or lobulated and do not involute.

Venous malformations are present at birth, are soft and compressible but bluish in colour, enlarge slowly with Valsalva manoeuvre, and do not have the bright red lobulated strawberry appearance or rapid postnatal growth.

Pyogenic granuloma is also a rapidly growing red vascular lesion but typically appears after minor trauma, ulcerates and bleeds easily, and occurs in older children and adults rather than at 4 months of age.
Glomuvenous malformations are present from birth, are painful with compression, bluish-purple, and do not exhibit the rapid postnatal growth phase of infantile haemangioma.
clinicalPearl: The key to distinguishing infantile haemangioma from vascular malformation is timing: haemangiomas are absent at birth and grow rapidly in infancy, while true vascular malformations are present from birth and grow proportionally.
keyClue: Absent at birth, rapid postnatal appearance and growth, bright red lobulated warm compressible strawberry lesion in a 4-month-old.
commonExamTrap: Confusing with port-wine stain because both are facial vascular lesions; port-wine stains are flat, macular, present from birth, and never lobulated or warm.
missedConcept: Haemangiomas undergo proliferation then involution (GLUT-1 positive endothelium); vascular malformations do not involute and are GLUT-1 negative — timing of onset is the key clinical differentiator.

topic: Capillary malformation (port-wine stain) — investigation for Sturge-Weber
subtopic: Syndromic associations of facial vascular malformations
clinicalStem: A 3-week-old male infant is referred to the plastic surgery and paediatric neurology team with a large unilateral port-wine stain covering the left forehead, upper eyelid, and lateral face in a V1 and V2 trigeminal distribution. The lesion was present at birth as a flat, macular, deep red patch that blancheson pressure. His parents also report occasional eye-rolling episodes. Which investigation is most important to arrange urgently?
options: Doppler ultrasound of the head and neck
CT angiography of the intracranial vessels
Punch biopsy of the port-wine stain
explanation: A port-wine stain in the V1 (ophthalmic) territory of the trigeminal nerve is associated with Sturge-Weber syndrome — leptomeningeal capillary-venous malformation causing seizures, intellectual disability, glaucoma, and hemiplegia. The episodic eye-rolling suggests seizure activity. MRI brain with gadolinium is the investigation of choice to detect leptomeningeal enhancement (pial angiomatosis) and early cortical changes, guiding anti-epileptic management. Early MRI allows neuro-protective treatment before irreversible cortical injury.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. V1-territory port-wine stain plus possible seizures demands urgent MRI brain with gadolinium to identify leptomeningeal involvement and guide anti-epileptic therapy in Sturge-Weber syndrome.

Plain skull X-ray may show 'tram-track' intracranial calcifications in established Sturge-Weber but these develop later in childhood and have poor sensitivity in infancy when early diagnosis and treatment matter most.
Doppler ultrasound is useful for haemangioma characterisation but cannot assess intracranial leptomeningeal involvement and is not appropriate for Sturge-Weber evaluation.
CT angiography visualises arterial anatomy rather than venous/capillary malformations; it exposes the infant to ionising radiation and contrast and is inferior to MRI for detecting pial angiomatosis.
Biopsy of a port-wine stain adds nothing in this clinical context; the diagnosis of the capillary malformation is clinical, and the urgent question is intracranial involvement, not skin histology.
clinicalPearl: Any facial port-wine stain involving the V1 (forehead/eyelid) distribution mandates urgent MRI brain with gadolinium to exclude leptomeningeal angiomatosis (Sturge-Weber syndrome) — early diagnosis prevents irreversible seizure-related cortical damage.
keyClue: V1-territory port-wine stain (forehead and upper eyelid) + episodic eye-rolling in a neonate = Sturge-Weber syndrome until excluded by MRI.
commonExamTrap: Choosing CT or skull X-ray, which are inferior to MRI for detecting the leptomeningeal enhancement that defines Sturge-Weber in infancy.
missedConcept: Sturge-Weber syndrome risk is specifically linked to V1 (ophthalmic division) port-wine stain distribution; V2/V3-only lesions carry much lower risk of intracranial involvement.
redFlagReason: The episodic eye-rolling in a neonate with a V1-territory port-wine stain represents likely seizures from Sturge-Weber leptomeningeal angiomatosis — delayed diagnosis leads to uncontrolled epilepsy and irreversible cortical injury.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old woman presents to the plastic surgery outpatient clinic with a soft, compressible, bluish swelling over her left masseter region that has been present since childhood and has slowly enlarged. The swelling increases in size when she bends forward or performs a Valsalva manoeuvre and diminishes in size when she lies down with the head elevated. There is no thrill, no bruit, and no warmth. Plain X-ray shows small calcifications within the lesion (phleboliths). Which single investigation is most useful for planning definitive treatment?
explanation: MRI with contrast, especially T2-weighted sequences, is the investigation of choice for venous malformations. Venous malformations appear as hyperintense lesions on T2 imaging, precisely delineating the extent of the malformation, the involvement of adjacent structures (parotid, masseter, pterygoid, mandible), and the presence of fluid-fluid levels from phleboliths. This information is essential for surgical planning or sclerotherapy. Phleboliths on X-ray are pathognomonic of a venous malformation but do not provide extent information.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. MRI T2 sequences show the full 3-dimensional extent of a venous malformation and its relationship to adjacent structures, which is indispensable for treatment planning.

Colour Doppler ultrasound confirms a low-flow venous malformation and shows compressibility but cannot adequately delineate depth, extent, or relationship to deep structures such as the mandible or parotid gland.

Digital subtraction angiography is used for high-flow vascular malformations (arteriovenous malformations) and arterial disease; it does not opacify venous malformations well and is not indicated here.

CT without contrast shows phleboliths well but cannot delineate the soft-tissue extent of the malformation, nor can it differentiate it from surrounding soft tissue without the signal characteristics provided by MRI.
Technetium-99m bone scan is used for osteoblastic activity and metastatic bone disease and has no role in the evaluation of soft-tissue vascular malformations.
clinicalPearl: MRI T2-weighted sequences are the gold standard for delineating venous malformations: the lesion is characteristically hyperintense on T2, enabling precise 3-D mapping for sclerotherapy or surgical planning.
keyClue: Soft compressible bluish swelling that increases with Valsalva, present since childhood, with phleboliths on X-ray = venous malformation requiring T2 MRI for treatment planning.
commonExamTrap: Choosing Doppler ultrasound as the definitive investigation; it confirms low-flow but cannot delineate the full anatomical extent needed for treatment planning.
missedConcept: Phleboliths on plain X-ray are pathognomonic of venous malformation but do not replace MRI for extent mapping; and Valsalva-dependent change is the key clinical sign of a low-flow venous malformation.
